sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Your new committee held its first meeting in mid – January, at the Red Lion in Burley in Wharfedale.  We welcomed our new Sites South Officer, Stephen Craven; and Comps Secretary, Ed Cleasby.  We hadn’t held a committee meeting for 4 months so there was plenty to talk about.

Only 7 members (of which 6 were on the committee) attended the Farmers’ Dinner back in October.  There were 29 farmers, which made it a fairly one-sided affair.  The committee agreed that we should aim to have at least 15 pilots present in future to balance things up.  Free tickets would appear to be the best incentive, but we haven’t quite decided how to allocate them.  Suggestions included first come, first served; a raffle; two for one; club coaches; and qualified first aiders.

The one day First Aid Course in early November was a great success.  All participants agreed that it was a good format and worthy of repeating, although perhaps better done in early spring rather than late autumn.  The club safety card will be amended to include locations in the what3words format.

The AGM achieved its aims.  The awards ceremony and the screening of ‘SOAR – a girls’ paragliding roadtrip’ added some colour to the evening.

We’re investigating the possibility of using MemberMojo to incorporate subscriptions and to record coaching details.  The former might cost the club an additional £130 per year, but save the membership secretary and treasurer a significant amount of time.  The latter costs nowt and would save the chief coach from having to generate separate lists.

The reserve repack took place on 18 Jan.  There is a full report elsewhere in this edition.  We only managed to fill 21 out of 40 places but our safety officer managed to negotiate a reduction in our fees so the club was only out of pocket to the tune of about £70.

There is no more space for engraving on the Northern Paragliding XC Cup, so our trophies’ officer has been authorised to replace it at a cost of £68.

Liam Toothill has taken over responsibility for maintaining the weather station but we’re still trying to work out how to transfer the direct debit for the sim card to the club’s bank account.

Sites (North) is in negotiations with the landowners about signs on the gate at Wether Fell, and the broken gate into the field at Semer Water.  Sites (South) has already made an impact by simplifying the arrangements at Windbank and obtaining a new landing field in Kettlewell (more in this edition).

Our librarian is ‘between houses’ so I’m temporarily in charge of the library.  Following the meeting we had a spring clean; and some of the older and less popular books will be going to the charity shop.  We’re also thinning out our collection of those old-fashioned DVD things – apparently not everybody has a DVD player these days.  I’m starting to feel old!

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

If there is sufficient interest I'll repeat my tour of sites local to Hawes on Sun 16 Feb 25 for novice pilots and anybody new to the club. The following timings seem to work pretty well:

1030 - Meet Dales Countryside Museum, Hawes

1120 - Tailbridge

1205 - Stags Fell

1315 - Semer Water (both faces)

1430 - Wether Fell

1530 - Dodd Fell

1720 - Sunset/debrief at The Fountain in Hawes

The aim is to bring the site guide to life by showing new (and potential) members a minimum of car parking, landing, route up, take-off and potential hazards. It's planned at this time of year in the likelihood that we won't be distracted by the opportunity to go flying. If it does look flyably then we'll re-evaluate the night before.

Meeting point at each site is the car park as marked in the Sites Guide and you're welcome to join/leave as you see fit. Dress for cold and wet although there will be more driving than walking (minimal). Bring your own butties and a flask.

PM me your phone number (no commitment) and I'll create a WhatsApp group to coordinate the day.  This invitation to the group might even work!

Pete Logan reports

Welcome to three new Club Coaches. Peter Darwood, Rosie Darwood and Alan Oliver. They’re located in Ilkey and Ribblesdale respectively. Contact details are on the website: https://www.dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2977 

Pete and Rosie have been flying for years and regularly go cross country, whilst Alan is a few years into flying and has just started the first few steps.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content: Clive Sury, Safety Officer reports

subsections.title: New Year’s Resolution?
subsections.content:
I don’t know how you may be keeping to any New Year’s resolutions but how about incorporating this late one?

Awareness of, and raising if required, incident reports. In December 2024 Skywings issued a notice regarding the importance of incident reporting. To save people clicking on a link to it, I’ve pasted a copy below.  Please take time to read this and more importantly, take note to raise incidents.

The online BHPA Incident Form can be found on their website with a quicklink here:

Dealing with and reporting incidents and accidents

As a reminder all incidents on DHPC sites should be reported!

DHPC members should report any incidents they are involved in at any UK or abroad site.  PLUS, send a copy of the pdf report generated to me as Safety Officer for the Club at safety@dhpc.org.uk 

Safe flying,

Clive

P.S.  Thanks to Andrew Nightingale for covering for me in co-ordinating activities on the day of the re-pack.  Plus, thank you to Guy Richardson and helpers, it looked like another busy and rewarding day.

Incident at Nonts Sarah

Earlier this year there was a collision between a paraglider and a model aircraft.  I am still awaiting a copy of the report for full details.  If anyone can get a copy of the report, please send it to me at safety@dhpc.org.uk 

Please note the site guide re aeromodellers:  

Aeromodellers tend to keep to the East of the site. Agree on this with those present before you fly, rather than arguing after a near miss.

subsections.title: Windbank
subsections.content:
We are changing the parking instructions for Windbank.  I have updated the site guide on our website

(https://www.dhpc.org.uk/site-guide/hawkswick-windbank-knipe-scar) although I still need to update the map.

Please could everyone please park at the lower end of the field from now on and keep well away from the woods at the top end as this is a sensitive area in the shooting season.

Thanks for your cooperation.

subsections.title: Kettlewell
subsections.content:
I am pleased to announce that we now have permission to land at the Cricket pitch at Kettlewell I will be updating

the site guide shortly..

Stephen Craven ( southern sites officer )

sections.title: Club Social Life
sections.content:
Stef Sykes reports

Seeing as I missed out on last month’s edition, I’d best make sure I get something in this time.

subsections.title: This month we meet on the 6th of Feb at the Horse and Farrier in Otley.
subsections.content:
We have special guests from the Yorkshire Gliding Club. These fellows use large fixed winged vehicles called ‘Sailplanes’ to access the air - god knows how it all works - but they’ll be coming along to let us know…

Martin will be there with the library.  You can see what’s available on the website.

Come at 7:30pm for a prompt 8pm start.

March the 6th sees fellow club member Paul bring us tales of flying in Bir.

subsections.title: Task Days
subsections.content:
This is a new idea, still in the planning stage and which I hope to trial over the coming season. What follows is the background, the thinking behind it and how I intend it to work (pending any small changes I may make).

Tasks days 2025

The NCS (Northern Challenge Series) is now entering its seventh season and the new BHPA guidance  means it now has to be Pilot rated and above only. My original intention was that it be seen as pilot development/progression, whether that be CP pilots near achieving their P rating, or new P rated pilots. My personal view was, and remains, that the step is significant and would benefit from a bit of smoothing … or put another way ‘gentle’ xc preparation skills.  Alongside the Pilot Task book (a new one is likely to be phased in, non-mandatory at the moment) … but the tasks will remain strictly ridge based and have very limited coverage of instrument, navigation, landing out skills etc. Whilst I regard the NCS tasks as ridge based, they aren’t quite ridge based enough to not fall foul of the tighter BHPA description.  Hence, I’m taking things down a notch with day tasks, so they do meet the new definition and if there’s a smidgen of doubt I (being a Senior Coach) am in a position to brief and sign off the declaration. Finally, not just the NCS, but other small, friendly events/comps are also looking to exclude CP rated pilots, so what follows is my way to address that at a very modest level, to have  fun at the same time and perhaps help get some of those CP tasks signed off in the process.

AIM: 

To introduce a small number of  ‘task days’ across the coming season aimed at latter stage CP/early P rated pilots.  They should be regarded as supplementary to other coaching activities the DHPC undertakes (or even part of a coaching day) but with the focus moving towards instrument and navigation using more extended CP flying tasks.

HOW IT WILL WORK:

A day and Site will be called at relatively short notice (48 hrs, maybe less). It will include a briefing time and location. Communication via the usual social media groups including our own forum.

The task will be set by me, taking account of the likely conditions, and shared via links and QR code. The link will take you to a private xcmap page for any downloads, uploads or visualisation of the task. After scoring the task will be deleted.

All sections of a task will be within sight or reach of a designated safe landing area.

There will be a full pre-task briefing covering all flight and safety aspects.  A debrief may be possible.

Pilots on the task will be monitored on ‘Puretrack’ – see image below as an example only.  I will set up a temporary task group to avoid clutter on a busy day. The group will be deleted after use. To be monitored you will require a feed into *‘Puretrack’ (from whatever instrument, app, tracker you use). It is free to use.

The winner of each task day will receive a task winner mug engraved with their name and task map image.

I mentioned at the beginning that this was by way of a trial. It’s always hard to gauge interest, but I expect numbers to be small. I wouldn’t advise fresh or low airtime (under 15 hrs) to be attempting these higher level tasks and I will be checking!  But for anyone feeling confident, with reasonable experience and wanting to push themselves a little it may be an opportunity to think about.

Some other comp stuff.

NCS – this opens on the 1st February.  Please read the amendment to the rules for 2025.

BPCup -  you can still register, but there is a waiting list and it’s currently  paid up at about 30 pilots.

LCC – this opens for registration on the 1st February. You will now need to be P rated or above by the time of the event 18- 22 June. The only exception will be the coaching group and limited to about 20 pilots.  Full details are available via the various telegram groups and CSC LCC website.

BCC – nothing has come back to me so for now I’ll take as no interest.

There are obviously many other intermediate comps out there and five UK based Hike and Fly events.  Too many to add here, but if you want any flagged up let me know..

Puretrack

Regarding *Puretrack (below). I’ve taken out an annual subscription for the PRO version - basically because it allows me to host tasks, set up groups and as a monitoring filter. One of these is the DHPC group. It’s currently non-password and anyone can join from the free version. It accepts data from many tracking sources and is easy to join. Please try it.

sections.title: Reserve Repack day in Ilkley
sections.content:
Andrew Nightingale reports

If you ever wanted to know what’s in your reserve bag and what it’s like to throw it, this was the place to be. Guy Richardson from Ginger Nomad gave a good introduction then demonstrated how to do a repack. Having a chance to throw the reserve and see it behave was a great confidence-builder: getting a feel for the resistance of pulling the handle and what it’s like trying to aim the reserve was particularly useful.

 

A few points from the talk

 

  • Most reserves are deployed at very low altitude “you’re never too low to throw” being the mantra
  • Square canopies resist rotation but rounds are slightly simpler to repack
  • A spare reserve bag and handle save you combing the countryside if you do end up deploying; especially handy if you’re going on holiday
  • Speaking of holidays, check if you need to provide a repack certificate to fly, in case you need to get it repacked professionally
  • Consider your make of harness when buying a reserve: there can be differences in bag size
  • If you deploy in a spin, throw the reserve in the opposite direction to reduce the risk of tangling

subsections.title: “Ooo. Stones and all”
subsections.content: One deployment liberated a few stones, and two reserves were found with line-overs, despite both having been professionally repacked the previous year (not with Ginger Nomad). This causes something affectionately known as a ‘Mae West’ on deployment, but it was easy to spot and quickly fixed (on the ground), just like dealing with a twisted riser.

subsections.title: “I’ve got it on a CD”
subsections.content: Printed repack instructions were the easiest and fastest to follow, saving around 20 mins repacking time. Reserve booklets usually have log books as well. Phone screens were too small but were good for being able to watch youtube videos.

subsections.title: The drop test
subsections.content:
Once the lines were stowed in the bag the final test was to lift the bag using the lines: if they slip out of the rubber band and the bag stays on the floor then it passes the drop test and gives you confidence that it will unravel nicely when deployed. One less thing to think about when flying.

 

Ginge’s team Ella, Bernard and Christina went above and beyond to help: Elastic bands, weights and bits of paracord were provided. They gave advice and help for unusual reserve shapes, and stayed back for nearly an hour to get everyone turned around.

Photos from Richard Cardwell

sections.title: Crash Dummy Confessions, 15 February, Ambleside
sections.content:
Martin Baxter relays

The CSC are hosting a safety talk in Ambleside on the 15th of February. The plan is for 9 pilots to tell one or two stories that are not just entertaining, but also have some theory and learning. The Dales club are welcome to attend at no cost.

Crash Dummy Confessions, Saturday 15th February, 10:00 - 16:00hrs, Ambleside Parish Centre.

The best accidents are experienced by someone else! Even better when those pilots are then willing to stand up and tell you what happened!  On Saturday 15th February nine pilots are going to talk about one or two paragliding accidents / incidents that are not just interesting, but also offer great learning opportunities.  Although this is a ‘safety’ meeting in disguise, I'm sure it will be a very entertaining and informative day for all pilots.

Please book a spot by text, WhatsApp or e-mail.

David Ashcroft

CSC Chief Club Coach

07752750869

canddashcroft@hotmail.com

sections.title: Library
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

As mentioned in my Chairman’s Chat, I’m the temporary custodian of the library.  As a consequence I’m slowly reviewing our collection of DVDs.  One that I enjoyed watching is an old 2001 film about Parahawking.  Not much point in keeping it because it’s available on Youtube here.  If you’re interested in parahawking you can participate, or get up to date information from The Parahawking Project website.

There is also a copy of Greg Hammerton’s film The Journey (2008) on Youtube.  It features a young and poetic Greg Hammerton ‘appearing to’ Vol Biv around the Western Cape, South Africa.  Whilst I was looking for an online version of the film I stumbled upon a review of the DVD in Skywords (p8) by Melise Harland who was our librarian back in Dec 2012.  She didn’t rate it!  I actually found the bit about the making of the film, and the effort involved, more interesting than the film itself.  That’s only available on the DVD version so we’ll be keeping a copy in the library, at least for the time being.  I note that BHPA is still flogging copies for £6!

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

One of the most useful functions of a GPS is a display of the wind direction and strength: something that you should be monitoring throughout your flight.  It is particularly useful when coming in to land at the end of an XC where the wind might be different to that where you took off, and there may not be any other indicators.  It can also be useful in judging the wind strength on the ridge (note that, at height when gale hanging, it’s sometimes difficult to know if your displayed single digit speed is forwards or backwards!).

The caveat is that a GPS will only give an accurate reading if you have completed at least one 360 degree turn (the more turns, the greater the accuracy).  This may be impossible in strong winds on the ridge, but here the direction is pretty obvious anyway.  On an XC you’ll have done lots of thermalling, but the end of it may well be marked by a long glide without any 360s.  When selecting a landing field try to leave plenty of time to do at least one 360 degree turn to confirm the wind direction.

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reflects

It doesn’t look like much has changed since 1988.  The BHPA’s current ratio of male to female members is about 16:1

Another thing that hasn’t changed is that Trev is still flying, although now soaring into his 80s he clearly isn’t quite as active as he was in 1989.  Joe’s Dad, John Edmonds, now a brave paraglider pilot, also gets a mention.  Silva compass in cloud eh Trev?

sections.title: Best of the Internet 🌐
sections.content:
Mike Barker reports

Improve Your Flying by Reading Clouds

Great video from Sebastian Benz - speeding up time/going back in time during one of his flights, to see the clouds develop and how he used them.

Lecture 1:  How to identify and name clouds (introduction to weather) A very detailed series of lectures from Mel Strong, University of New Mexico. Worth book marking for when the weather keeps us on the ground…advanced meteorology, but really interesting!

https://xcmag.com/news/gear-news/cloudbase-foundation-old-gliders-for-good-causes/

Old glider, avoiding landfill, clearing some space and a good cause.
